title: Foot numbness
source_review_basis: committed exact-section summaries and evidence records; full guideline documents were not retained for this pass

examination.text: Supports sudden unilateral numbness, rapidly progressive symmetrical sensory change with weakness or imbalance, recurrent sensory episodes, distal sensory distribution, reflex and gait context, selected metabolic tests, radicular symptoms, bowel or bladder context, and clinician-led referral.
examination.rationale: The exact adult sensory-symptom section supports onset, distribution, unilateral or symmetrical pattern, progression, weakness, imbalance, gait, reflex, back or radicular, bowel or bladder, metabolic-test, and referral context without assigning neuropathy. The exact neurological section supports recording existing metabolic, neurological, or imaging results and clinician-entered follow-up or referral status without generating tests.
examination.evidence_extract: Supports sudden unilateral numbness, rapidly progressive symmetrical sensory change with weakness or imbalance, recurrent sensory episodes, distal sensory distribution, reflex and gait context, selected metabolic tests, radicular symptoms, bowel or bladder context, and clinician-led referral.

source.title: Suspected neurological conditions: recognition and referral

exact_section.heading: Recommendations 1.10.1–1.10.13 — sensory symptoms including tingling or numbness in adults

examination.text: Supports documenting age-appropriate review, bilateral skin and sensory examination, perfusion, ulceration, callus, infection or inflammation, deformity, gangrene, Charcot features, and clinician-entered risk classification without calculating it.
examination.rationale: The exact diabetic-foot section supports clinician-performed bilateral skin, sensory, perfusion, ulcer, callus, infection, deformity, gangrene, and Charcot assessment only when diabetes and examination context apply.
examination.evidence_extract: Supports documenting age-appropriate review, bilateral skin and sensory examination, perfusion, ulceration, callus, infection or inflammation, deformity, gangrene, Charcot features, and clinician-entered risk classification without calculating it.

source.title: Diabetic foot problems: prevention and management

exact_section.heading: Recommendations 1.3.1–1.3.6 — frequency, bilateral foot examination, and clinician risk stratification

source_limitations: Laterality, distribution, onset, weakness, gait, diabetes, vascular status, spine symptoms, examination, cause, diagnosis, test, treatment, referral, and urgency remain unsupported.
Any legacy item not explicitly mapped to an exact section remains unsupported pending qualified clinician review.
No mapping authorises an automatic diagnosis, differential, investigation, management decision, medicine, dose, procedure, referral, urgency conclusion, discharge decision, or patient instruction.

All previously supported GP legacy-item mappings were conservatively removed because exact item identity, direct support, and item-specific applicability were not independently established under the fail-closed contract.
curation_policy: Direct guideline curation: retain only explicitly mapped content; rewrite only with explicit item mapping; remove unmapped legacy content; add only committed exact-section evidence summaries.
